text: "I am Mugasha-a-a-a."
He said, "Come grab hold . . . The water is swallowing me, Mugasha!"
That day, my lords, we fell into a quandary.
All nine women there fell into a quandary.
Confounded they were.
That day, Nyatende and Nyambubi:
A woman there they call Nyatende,
A sister of Nyambubi.
The woman they call Nyatende,
Sister of Nyambubi
That day she spoke to Nyambubi,
"Child of my mother, ancestral spirit
Help me place my feet for mercy's sake
So I can grab my child!"
She said, "So I can grab my child.
This very day, I will.
"You know that if an only child dies,
It kills the one that bore it;
It brings us haunting;
It returns and brings bad dreams as well.
Mugasha's mother's sister waded out in the water.
It grabbed her to the nipples of her breasts.
The woman backed out buttocks first that day.
The woman raised her hand as though she laid a curse.
I saw her speak to Mugasha.
She said, "I am not the one who gave you birth,
O dear, not I who pushed for you.
Let not the water swallow me, Mugasha!"
The woman backed out buttocks first that day.
Since Mugasha had no one to take him from the lake,
That day he got out from the lake himself.
He got back in the canoe.
And when he reached the shore,
He found them fighting over fish,
People of squatting and of iron,
You know, people of hoes ruled by kabagala,
They came to fight.
They quarreled over an enkuyu fish,
Big eyes skyward like a forgetful debtor.
The one said, "I am the one who gave him birth,"
She said, "I am the one who pushed for him."
The other said, "If you gave birth to him, then why
When he fell into the water did you not go grab him?
The one said, "And why
When you went to grab him, did you back out buttocks first?"
Ewewe yo o
It happened that Wamala was there that day.
He showed them the copper spear of Kagolo,
Missle that causes envy,
Dry words spoken in fury
Stiff elbows,
All adorned in beads except the palm,
Of the left-handers,
Of the water oracle;
He drank it–he spilled it,
He built it–he burned it,
Pouncer on warriors.
Wamala said, "You nine women, as you are here,
Make a judgement, my children:"
He said, "If a warrior woman steps forward and spears Mugasha and kills him,
I will give her nine children."
And to show how much they want to reproduce, my lord,
The People of the Peaceful-one:
Mugasha's mother's sister said, "Let me spear Mugasha. I'll kill him.
They'll give me the nine children.
These cannot be compared to the single child of Nyambubi."
E wewe e
When she raised the spear, Nyambubi leapt up from below.
She whirled and grabbed her,
And she said, "Why should this thing kill my child?
I didn't cause its barrenness."
That day those present said, "Nyambubi, you win the dispute.
No woman would kill a child she gave birth to.
Nyambubi, the child is yours."
Then they gave her a large fish,
An enkuyu, big eyes skyward like a forgetful debtor.
Of the woman Nyatende, sister of Nyambubi
They said–women, I tell you, sad but true–
"Mother's sisters are people with ill wishes.
That woman, don't leave her empty handed."
They give her little obukeije and enkolongo fish
E wewe e Mugasha
He left, having found his mother, who gave him birth.
He returned to Great Lake Lwelu, the trapper did.
He spent four-and-four-make-eight days there
He then called Rushing-water.
E-e, of the lake, you-u
"You, Rushing-water of the lake,
Whirling one, child of limbtwister,
You pulled out the fish lines."
He said, "This very day I am wishing:
I would visit all the lakeshore landings
To the mouths of rivers I would go."
He said, "I would go to the mouth
Where the Kagera opens onto Great Lake Lwelu.
I would pass by there to Ishulaine Bugunda."
The commotion they created pulled out all the fish traps on the lake.
That day they paddled hard.
He turned at Kishanje village of Kalasha at Mutailenge's,
He went to Ibebe Bweza's.
Lubafu at the waters of Nyamataba at Kanyabagwa beach.
He landed at Kyahu Island of Nyakwezi.
He cast his eyes toward Bitterness Marsh.
It was then he saw eight maidens.
And the ninth was Lyanyaibungo, bull of the kalaito.
The child happened to be gathering straw–so far, eight bundles.
The ninth she held in her hand.
The tenth she pressed underfoot.
The child was surprised by whistling-wind,
Blower of cold,
I never miss a doorway.
Silent runner, lightning-flash,
The child was surprised by thunder (Kaiyula).
She was surprised by winged nshungu wind of Lwabulungu,
Great horns lie back across the shoulders,
Bad news brought by a traveler.
She was surprised by lake-wind, water-bundler,
Mwigundu wind of Bitoma village,
Associated with the swaying-one of Nyante.
Her gathered straw, the winds flung round and round.
With her raphia skirt she tried and tried to push the bundles down, my lord,
But without effect.
With her raphia skirt she tried and tried to push the bundles down,
But without effect.
She sat down on the ground.
She swore, "O my father!
Swaying-one of Nyante."
And she had scarcely gotten the words from her mouth,
When sudenly Mugasha appeared.
He said, "Maiden, let me tell you clearly:
I am the only child.
I ended my mother's barrenness.
But now, I would court you. I would marry you.
I am a child of the Kagulamo springs.
He said, "I am a child of hailstones, of earth tremors,
I come from the blocker."
The maiden wept. She wailed.
She spat.
Her spittle scattered.
I saw the child press and press her things together.
She sat confounded.
She said, "But, Mugasha, let me tell you clearly, too:
You cannot marry me,
You little fisherman of Ishulaine Bugunda
She said, "Die without issue! You stink!"
That day Mugasha had tied on his cloak of dried minnows.
The maiden on that day,
She said, "Just let me swear something to you, trapper"
She said, "Let me tell you this, Mugasha:
Were you to marry me,
I'd as well lie with Kagolo, the child of my own mother,
She said, "Of the spirit shrine,
Of the water oracle,"
She said, "Missle that causes envy,
Dry words spoken in fury,
Of stiff elbows,
All adorned in beads except the palm,
Of the left-handers
Of the water oracle,
He drank it–he spilled it;
He built it–he burned it,
Pouncer on warriors."
The maiden that day swore to the trapper
That day he spoke, the water-dweller,
Child of the Kagulamo springs
He said, "And you, welata,"
He said, "Welata, welata," [.]
[He said, "Rushing-water of the Lake, (Puller) of Fish-lines,"
He said, "Whirling one, child of Limbtwister,
Maiden, and I swear to you, welata,"
"Were I not to marry you,
I'd undress and show my nakedness, welata."
He said,"Rushing-water of the Lake, (puller) of fish lines,"
He said, "Whirling one, child of limbtwister,
"Go, I'll put them in the king's forecourt, welata,
Go, sound my great drum,
They-die But-they've-been-told, welata
I'll also put some beer in there to go."
O oh
Wewe eh
E wewe eh
E weh the trapper
Eh?
That day they sounded the drum, my lord, They-die But-they've-been-told
Roaring dididi
Roaring dididi
Roaring dididi
Roaring dididi
Roaring dididi
Roaring dididi
Roaring dididi
Roaring dididi
Roaring dididi
It spoke, They-die But-they've-been-told, my lord.
First to arrive was Nkuyu fish,
Big eyes skyward like a forgetful debtor.
I'll tell you well: Mugasha's men began to attend
First to arrive was Nkuyu fish,
Big eyes skyward like a forgetful debtor.
He said, "But Lyanyaibungo, Mugasha
Trapper, they'll give her to you.
You marry her, trapper!"
Hippopotomus, smasher of canoes, elder of the waves,
Said, "When I arrive at the home of the swaying-one of Nyante
I'll block the doorway, trapper."
I saw the crocodile say, "You marry her, Mugasha!
Pangolins say, "You marry her, trapper!"
The otters say, "You marry her, Mugasha!"
Obushwaikogoto fishes say, "You marry her, trapper!
I saw the obukeije fish, the speeders.
They say, "That day you marry her, Mugasha!"
Enkolongo fish, small horns, enkoma mujungwizi,
Enkolongo said, "When I sit by the door,
Whoever passes carelessly,
I'll raise my spine a bit,
And I'll pierce him, Mugasha."
"But should you fail to marry Nyaibungo, Mugasha,
We'd drink at the spearshaft of Ndekezi."
E wewe eh
E wewe eh
He said, "Now we'd drink at the spearshaft of Ndekezi."
The lungfish said, "You marry her, Mugasha!"
He arose and praised himself, Kasholobana Ekihumpi
It said, "But Lyanyaibungo, Mugasha,
Trapper, they'll give her to you.
You marry her, trapper"
He arose and prasised himself that day, the catfish.
He said, "You marry her, Mugasha!"
Then he arose and spoke, the ensenene grasshopper.
He said, I am Lwakyenge and Bagaza
My head is a straw my foot njulujunga
"I'll fall four-and-four-make-eight days in Kyamutwala
On the ninth day they'll be harvesting in bushel baskets.
"But should you fail to marry Lyanyaibungo, Mugasha,
Trapper, we'd drink at the spearshaft of Ndekezi.
E wewe o o
On that day, poor boy, he arose and praised himself, the Grasshopper.
He said, I am the sharpening stone
Lover of the mountain pass
I'll fall for three whole days
The tree of sacrifices you see behind the house of the swaying-one of Nyante,
Muhata, I'll pull up by the roots, trapper."
He arose and praised himself on that day, thunder, essential warrior,
He said, "When I strike in the forecourt,
Then strike behind the house,
He said, "Some will die,"
He said, "Others will merely faint.
But Lyanyaibungo, Mugasha, they'll give her to you.
You marry her, trapper!"
He arose and praised himself on that day,
Whistling-wind, with others, blower of cold, I do not miss a doorway
They said, "When we cut banana trees at the swaying-one of Nyante's house, Mugasha,
They'll give her to you. You marry her, trapper!"
Hailstones, child of Earthquake
Said, "On that day you marry her, Mugasha!"
That day, my lord, while we were there
That day he spoke, the Water-dweller,
Child of the springs of Kagulamo.
He said, "Welata, welata."
He said, "Rushing-water of the Lake, (puller) of fish lines,
Have you carried them, welata?"
They said, "Yes."
"Have you carried them, welata?"
They said, "Yes."
"Rushing-water of the Lake, (puller) of fish lines,"
He said, "Have you carried them welata?"
They said, "Yes."
He said, "Rushing-water of the Lake, (puller) of fish lines,
Take what's needed for a marriage feast at Wamala's, welata.
I don't go to propose.
I go directly to the wedding feast, welata."
Rushing-water said, "My lord,"
He said, "When they go to marry at the house of Wamala,
Swaying-one of Nyante, my lord,"
He said, "They present cattle."
He said, "Welata, take hippopotomi."
They said, "But my lord, goats?
He said, "We'll take crocodiles."
They say, "A hunting dog?"
He said, "Take an otter."
They said, "An iron bell?"
He said, "A tortoise."
They said, "A leash?"
He said, "A luzilamili snake."
They said, "A braided collar?"
He said, "Msuliti ashweela nyali awelata"
They said, "My lord, and when we've found these things . . ."
They said, "A maiden of the hafted knife to enter the feast?"
He said, "Take an eel."
They said, "An old woman to block the way?"
He said, "Take a kasolobana fish."
They said, "My lord, and when we've found these things . . ."
"Women to sing keening songs?"
He said, "Take lungfish."
"An elder to make the speech?"
He said, "Take an enkolongo fish."
Wewe wewe
Wo o
Ewe we
Wamala, swaying-one of Nyante,
In the forecourt he stood confounded.
He saw in the forecourt, poor man . . .
That day he was surprised by frogs, jumping and jumping.
They sang, "They'll tell of this, they'll tell of this."
They said, "They'll tell of this, they'll tell of this."
They said, "What did I tell you, father of grunters?
Release me, and I'll release you.
What did I tell you, bloodbrother of mine?
Release me, and I'll release you.
Yo o father of grunters,
Release me,
E eh Bloodbrother of mine,
Release me, and I'll release you."
They said, "What did I tell you, father of grunters?
Release me, and I'll relea . . .
E eh bloodbrother of mine,
Release me, and I'll release you.
And my lord, when he went and looked into the reception room,
Wamala, swaying-one of Nyante,
He saw the enkolongo fish.
It had sat in the reception room and was making its speech.
Now, my lord, what did Wamala begin to do then?
To transform Mugasha's things
By his power.
He saw nlolongo had sat and was making a speech.
Nkolongo's spear placed on his shoulder to enter the feast,
Became a spine stuck to his back,
By the power of Wamala.
The elder woman who blocks the way became a kasolobana ekihumpi fish.
You know how they enter a feast:
One elder woman goes to the back room, another, to the front.
They answer one another singing.
They toss songs back and forth in cackling voices.
One they do: "Mine is a spoiled child.
When she sees the fallow land she cultivates."
(Audience:) Mercy yes
The elder woman was a kasolobana ekihumpi fish,
And long ago the ekihumpi had a mouth like other fish.
Now as she sat to cackle out those words,
Her mouth stretched longer,
By the power of Wamala.
The women who sang the keening songs were lungfish.
Long ago lungfish were white-white-white like cloth.
But the house they sat in was a "Let's pray to see at dawn he's made it through the night."
Below was kerosene.
Above was gasoline.
It confounds one.
Now the soot that dripped down,
Was what stuck spots on them,
By the power of Wamala
The maiden of the hafted knife was catfish.
And you know the maiden of the hafted knife–
They rub more fat on her than on the bride herself.
They place the hafted knife on her. She sits motionless.
The maiden was an eel.
The slipperyness of the fat they spread on her,
Now means when you go to grasp a catfish,
You first must pat a bit of mud,
By the power of Wamala.
Wo o
Wamala stood there confounded.
He said, "I cannot give a daughter to the fisherman from Ishulaine Bugunda.
I am Wamala, swaying-one of Nyante,
My child cannot be married to a fisherman.
Then Mugasha sent Whistling-wind, among others,
They cut down banana palms . . . without effect.
You hear them say, "In such-and-such a place, the wind has cut them, but in another place, it has cut even
And in still another place, it merely passed through.
Now on that day, no wind came to the neighbors.
To the neighbors it did come but passed through gently, gently.
At Wamala's house,
Even plantain sprouts of bottle length were felled.
He still refused, the swaying-one of Nyante.
He sent thunder, essential warrior.
He struck in the forecourt, he struck behind the house.
Do you remember what it's like when thunder strikes at your neighbor's?
Thunder struck at Wamala's in the forecourt; he struck behind the house.
Some died, others merely fainted.
They splashed water on them and they revived.
But Wamala still refused, saying "I cannot marry my daughter to a fisherman."
He sent hailstones, child of earthquake.
Wamala said,"My child,"
He said, "The way he goes to war amazes."
Can you imagine what a hailstone big enough to grab is like?
Now Wamala stood there
Covered from feet to chest.
Above was only head and arms,
His legs and clothing sunk in hailstones.
He said, "Get a slave girl. Dress her as a bride.
Let the bull of Nyambubi marry her."
He said, "But he will not marry my daughter, Lyanyaibungo,
Bull of the kalaito."
When they left the child on the threshold by the wicker screen,
When hippo looked and said, "She's not the one we saw in the marsh!"
When hippo spoke to her gruffly, "Ha! ha! ha! hu! hu!"
The maiden jumped and ran back to the kitchen pots.
She sat there saying, "This is not my affair."
They brought Lyanyaibungo, Bull of the kalaito.
They rubbed her with ashes and charcoal and burnt grass.
They said, "He won't know her now, Mugasha."
When they set her out in the reception room
Rushing-water came and picked her up.
He brought her to meet him on Nykilembeka beach.
He said, "That's the one, welata welata."
He said, "You've traveled far, welata."
They said, "Indeed."
He said, "You've travelled far, welata."
They said, "Indeed."
He said, "You've travelled far, welata."
They said, "Indeed."
He said, "And when you got there, did you meet Kagolo?,
Of the spirit shrine,
Of the water oracle, welata?"
They said, "We did not meet him there."
He said, "Paddle quickly!"
Don't you know Kagolo?
He comes to churn the lake up
While the children are still in the middle."
He said, "Paddle quickly, welata!
Don't you know Kagolo?
He comes to churn the lake up
While the children are still in the middle, welata."
Wo wo

Ya ya
He said, "Paddle quickly, welata!
Don't you know Kagolo?
He comes to churn the lake up
While the children are still in the middle, welata."
Ya ya
O o
Ya ya
He said, "Rushing-water, Mpondele of fish lines,
Nseli nkakabego ka kabantende
Tomali ngonatuka
Nkenseli nkakabego ka kabantende welata
Paddle quickly! Don't you know Bwemwatuka?
O o
Ya ya
We we
Ya ya
That day, my lord, Mugasha, I tell you,
He kept her inside for four-and-four-make-eight days.
That day he married her, the trapper.
He kept her in for four-and-four-make-eight days.
Suddenly, there appeared Kagolo,
.Of the water oracle,
Missle that causes envy,
He'd not been in.
He was in Buganda of Ntege and Kahimbo.
He was in Buzinza of Nshanshame
He had gone to trade, Kagolo.
When he returned, he asked his father Wamala,
Swaying-one of Nyante
He said, "Father, where is our child Lyanyaibungo,
Bull of the kalaito?"
He said, "Tell me, for I was away.
I was in Buganda of Ntege and Kihimbo.
I was in Buzinza of Nshanshame.
I had gone to trade."
We we
His father said, "My child,"
He said, "Just look at my plantation.
Look how even now the banana palms have not stood up."
He said, "Look also at the things Mugasha left,
My child,"
He said, "Those fish were once inside."
They were picking up and throwing out those left by the waters. [some indistinct words]
He said, "Father,"
He said, "I ask a question
What did you do then?"
He answered, "Mugasha took her."
He said, "Father,
I ask a single question–
On this earth how many kings are you?"
He said, "We are two: I and Mugasha."
He said, "And what do you rule, Wamala?"
He said, "I rule the land."
He said, "And Mugasha?"
He said, "He rules the lake."
He said, "Land or lake–which is greater?"
He said, "My child,
Land is the greater; lake, the smaller.."
And as Wamala said, I reckon that it's true:
Travellers to the Indian Ocean say the water rests entirely on the land.
Or is it in the air?
True or wrong?
Ewe we
He said, "My child land is the greater; water, the smaller, but he was . . ."
He said, "Give me leave to go after I recite my praise.
My mother's child cannot be married by the fisherman of Ishulaine Bugunda."
He said, "I am Kagolo
Of the spirit shrine,
Of the water oracle,
Missle that causes envy,
I am dry words spoken in fury,
I, of the stiff elbows
All adorned in beads except the palm,
Of the left-handers,
Of the water oracle,"
He said, "I drank it–I spilled it;
I built it –I burned it,
Pouncer on warriors."
He said, "Give me my bow."
They gave him one of coffee wood.
When Kagolo went out to try it in the courtyard, it snapped.
He returned.
He grabbed one of mulinzi wood.
When he went out to the courtyard, it snapped.
Kagolo returned.
He grabbed one of mutengo wood.
When he went out to try it in the courtyard, it snapped
Kagolo returned.
He grabbed one of mukanshe wood.
Then he grabbed eight arrows.
The ninth went in his hand,
Making a clacking sound, "enjenje."
Along the path that took him were banana plants.
They were transformed to raphia palms,
By the power of Kagolo.
When he reached the cattle road,
Mishunshu shrubs were transformed to bijeela,
By the power of Kagolo.
He reached a mujumbo.
It was transformed to a coffee tree,
By the power of Kagolo.
He arrived...
He arrived outside the village.
Shanje grass was transformed to yojwe,
By the power of Kagolo.
You see these plants resemble one another.
Banana tree resembles raphia palm,
If you stand a way off from its dried foliage.
Mushunshu resembles kijela,
If you stand a way off from its dried foliage.
Mujumbo resembles coffee's dried foliage.
Ewewe iyoo
Ewewe iyoo
He went and stood on a boulder at Nyakilembeka.
He looked into the middle of the lake and saw Mugasha.
He was catching bukeije and nkolongo fish.
The helmsman of the canoe was Rushing-water.
Then, my lords, to concentrate my telling,
My friends, to make its details clear–
Kagolo stood in about this spot.
Mugasha was far off on the lake, like the distance to Itahwa. (several miles)
He looked and said, "If I let fly at him from here,
The arrows will not reach.
The wind will throw them off."
He called to him, "You, Mugasha,
Come and take me.
I would see our child Lyanaibungo,
Bull of the kalaito.
The reason being, I was not there.
I was in Buganda of Ntege and Kiimbo.
I was in Buzinza of Nshanshame.
I had gone to trade.
Now come and take me, please.
I would see our child Lyanyaibungo."
Mugasha trained one eye on him.
The helmsman of the canoe was Rushing-water.
He said, "Rushing-water of the Lake, (puller) of fish lines,
Olyatotombola welata
We've not finished and Kagolo
Comes to churn the lake up.
While the children are still in the middle."
Ewe wewe e
The canoe floated even further into the middle of the lake.
Now, it left Itahwa
And arrived at about Ibula or Magoti.
[Mugasha, there . . .]
When he saw that the canoe had floated off a bit,
Kagolo stood up on the rocky outcrop.
He truly let eight arrows fly,
But as they came over the lake,
The wind was tossing them about.
For the ninth, he lay prone upon the rock.
He let it fly
With all his strength, for it was the last one he had.
It travelled and it stuck in Mugasha's knee.
His own leg–Mugasha lopped it off.
He threw it in the lake.
He went inside the Mugoma rocky outcrop.
He brought out Lyanyaibungo,
Bull of the kalaito.
He said, "Let's go. I'll take you.
Your people have come for you."
He said, "I cannot marry you.
I am disfigured in my leg, Mugasha."
He said, "But for eight days I was married to you, Lyanyaibungo,
Bull of the kalaito,
Should you bear a child, it would be mine."
When he took about two paddle strokes,
He felt great pain, the trapper.
He summoned thunder, essential warrior,
He said, "I would meet Kagolo at the landing.
He'll have no word to say."
He said, "But do not stop his heart."
When he let loose a single bolt,
It struck the solid rock–which split apart.
From the place Kagolo stood,
He sank suddenly into Kabyantabuzi rock.
Only his head and hands remained above.
When he reached there, Mugasha,
He said, "You, Kagolo,
Look, your family's child is here, Lyanyaibungo,
Bull of the kalaito."
He answered, "Ti ti ti ti ti ti."
"Kagolo of the spirit shrine,
I, Mugasha, have come."
He said, "Ti ti ti ti ti."
With his paddle, Mugasha dealt Kagolo's head a blow–
Which launched and freed him from Kabyantabuzi rock.
His word to Kagolo at the landing was this:
"Whoever hits you in the eye– you hit him in the nose."
Mugasha had just one leg to stand on.
And since that time what did Kagolo do?
He's never found a word to say.
